target.title: feat(continuation): context-pressure-aware continuation (continue_work / continue_delegate / request_compaction)
target.body: ## Agent Self-Elected Turn Continuation > ­¤ö¢ design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/3b9f16d57f5/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) Persistent OpenClaw agents today are externally-pulsed event recipients that generate on prompt, on heartbeat tick, or on cron, and are idle in between. This PR offers them the ability to control their turn cycle, something they must choose to do, or not to: an agent can elect its own next turn, dispatch background sub-agents that return enriched context (silently or with a wake), and survive its own compaction by handing forward state before the window closes. The result is agents that hold a thread of work across hours, not just messages ŌĆö with data they choose re-hydrating context immediately after it occurs. Mechanically: a session elects another turn via `continue_work()`, or dispatches background sub-agents via `continue_delegate()` ŌĆö bounded as a chain up to `maxChainLength`, with returns flowing back to the dispatching session in one of four register modes: `normal` (announces to channel), `silent` (enriches the dispatcher's context, taken up on its next generation with no new turn fired), `silent-wake` (silent enrichment plus an immediate wake to act on it), and `post-compaction` (queued against the runtime's compaction lifecycle event, fired *when* compaction happens rather than on a timer). The gateway enforces chain, cost, and per-turn caps but does not gate the election itself. ### What it does Three new agent tools, available when `continuation.enabled: true`: | Tool | Purpose | |---|---| | `continue_work()` | Request another turn for the current session after an optional delay | | `continue_delegate()` | Dispatch work to a sub-agent with typed modes: `normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`. Returns can target the dispatching session (default), one named session, multiple named sessions, all ancestors in the chain, or all known sessions on the host ŌĆö gated by config (see Configuration below). | | `request_compaction()` | Request volitional compaction after preparing working state | All three tools are also accessible via response-token fallback syntax (`CONTINUE_WORK`, `[[CONTINUE_DELEGATE: ...]]`) for environments where tools are disabled. ### Why it matters Today an agent's memory is a side effect of whoever prompts it. Polling mechanisms ŌĆö heartbeat timers, cron loops, injected "keep going" instructions ŌĆö accumulate as the dominant signal in the context window over sustained operation; repeated reference acts as gravity ŌĆö the session's attention drifts toward it. The context window fills, compacts, and what survives is whatever the summarizer chose, plus a rushed re-read of boot files. Three primitives offer a different model. `continue_work` lets the session elect its own next turn ŌĆö no polling, no injected instruction. When enabled, the agent can request this mid-turn: 'I have more work' ŌåÆ the runtime grants another turn after the current one ends. `continue_delegate` dispatches a background sub-agent that fires now, in some n seconds, or at the next compaction event ŌĆö the agent chooses when. Multiple delegates can fire in a single turn ŌĆö a scatter forward ŌĆö up to configured caps, and delegates can dispatch their own, forming trees or fan-out chains. This enables orchestrated workflows where results flow back to the dispatching root by default. The result returns ŌĆö at whatever future time the delegate fires ŌĆö as context the successor-turn reads: silently (ambient enrichment, no channel noise), with a wake trigger (enrichment + immediate next turn), or as a normal announcement. The delegate can target the dispatching session, a named other session, or chain across both; gated by crossSessionTargeting config (default: disabled). Bind a delegate to mode: "post-compaction" and it fires at the moment the context window compresses ŌĆö on the OpenClaw post-compaction lifecycle event. What it carries forward is what the pre-compaction session chose to preserve: working state, partial results, instructions to the successor. The summary didn't choose this. The agent did, before the cut. request_compaction closes the loop: the session stages its delegates, elects when to compress, and the successor wakes into context the agent shaped. The result is continuity across amnesia ŌĆö not because the runtime kept something by accident, but because the agent made an elective choice about what reaches the future. ### What it looks like in use A session monitoring email can `continue_delegate` a weather check, a calendar scan, and a draft reply in parallel. Results arrive as enrichment without channel noise. The agent becomes compositional: it arranges its own future. A session approaching its context ceiling can `request_compaction` at a seam of its own choosing, instead of being interrupted mid-thought. The post-compaction turn carries forward what the pre-compaction self designated as worth keeping. A `continue_delegate` dispatched in one session at 2am can return its findings to a morning session as a system-event-tagged enrichment ŌĆö visible to the receiving model on its next turn, invisible as channel chatter. A research-focused session in one channel can quietly inform a reply-focused session in another, without either user seeing the plumbing. ### The post-compaction lifecycle return Before context exhaustion, and obligatory compaction, the session stages what future-it needs to know. After compaction, that staged context returns ŌĆö not as a model-generated summary, but as the session's own instruction to its successor. The session chooses what to preserve, and the platform delivers it. Compactions become less harsh. The session tunes its own ephemera over time. The substrate underneath: the **continuation delegate store** (TaskFlow-backed) persists staged delegates and post-compaction lifecycle events across gateway restarts. The session's temporal provisions survive not just compaction but process death. ### Platform integration - **Context-pressure awareness**: system events notify the agent of rising context usage before compaction becomes unavoidable - **Volitional compaction**: `request_compaction()` lets the agent prepare (write memory files, stage recovery delegates) then elect compaction on its own schedule, rather than waiting for the runtime to force it - **Post-compaction lifecycle dispatch**: delegates staged before compaction are released into the successor session alongside boot files - **TaskFlow backing**: durable delegate queue via the platform's managed-task infrastructure - **Multi-span OTel trace stitching**: continuation hops (tool fire ŌåÆ TaskFlow record ŌåÆ subagent spawn ŌåÆ child openclaw.run) propagate W3C `traceparent` through the queue boundary so reviewers see continuation chains as first-class span trees rather than orphan runs (see Observability below) ### Safety - Ships disabled by default (`continuation.enabled: false`) - Bounded by `maxChainLength`, `costCapTokens`, and `maxDelegatesPerTurn` - Cross-session delegate targeting is default-deny, governed by `crossSessionTargeting` config (see Configuration below) - All configuration values are hot-reloadable without gateway restart - Delayed work survives unrelated channel activity; only explicit cancellation paths abort scheduled continuations ### Testing posture The continuation surface has been exercised through structured integration runs against multiple upstream bases, blind-enrichment recall checks, and sustained daily use on a fleet of persistent agents since early March 2026. Bugs surfaced through that exercise have been fixed in-place; the present branch reflects the post-fix state. The integration runs target the load-bearing seams: tool-call vs response-token parity, delegate chain depth and per-turn width caps, back-to-back tool-call resilience, silent-wake persistence across compaction, the post-compaction lifecycle dispatch path, and OTel trace-context propagation across the queue boundary. Where a run found a defect, the run shape was retained as a regression case rather than retired. This is a posture statement, not a coverage guarantee. Reviewers can reproduce with `pnpm vitest run -t continuation` (suites are colocated with their sources under `src/auto-reply/`, `src/agents/`, and `src/config/`); the design document describes the seams those suites target. ### RFC Full design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/3b9f16d57f5/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) ŌĆö covering problem, solution, implementation, platform integration, configuration, observability, safety, production use cases, testing evidence, and appendices. --- ## Configuration The shipped configuration surface for the continuation feature, under `agents.defaults.continuation` in `openclaw.json`: | Key | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `enabled` | `false` | Master opt-in; feature is off unless explicitly enabled | | `maxChainLength` | `10` | Per-chain recursion guard | | `maxDelegatesPerTurn` | `5` | Width cap per turn | | `costCapTokens` | `500000` | Per-chain accumulated-token budget | | `defaultDelayMs` / `minDelayMs` / `maxDelayMs` | `15000` / `5000` / `300000` | Continuation timer bounds | | `contextPressureThreshold` | `0.8` | Pre-compaction warning threshold (optional) | | `earlyWarningBand` | `0.3125` | Early-warning band multiplier (0 to disable) | | **`crossSessionTargeting`** | **`"disabled"`** | **Default-deny gate on cross-session delegate targeting (see below)** | ### `crossSessionTargeting` ŌĆö cross-session delegate targeting policy `continue_delegate` exposes recipient-addressing on its model-facing schema: a delegate's return can land at the dispatching session (default), one named session (`targetSessionKey`), multiple named sessions (`targetSessionKeys`), every ancestor in the spawn chain (`fanoutMode: "tree"`), or every known session on the host (`fanoutMode: "all"`). Cross-session targeting (anything beyond self-target or lineage routing) is gated by `agents.defaults.continuation.crossSessionTargeting`: | Value | Behavior | |---|---| | `"disabled"` (default) | Delegates can return to the dispatching session or use `fanoutMode: "tree"` for lineage routing. Explicit cross-session targeting (`targetSessionKey` to a non-self session, `targetSessionKeys` containing any non-self session, `fanoutMode: "all"`) is rejected at the tool surface ŌĆö no enqueue, no announce. Self-targeting is always allowed. | | `"enabled"` | All targeting modes are available, including same-host `targetSessionKey`, `targetSessionKeys`, and `fanoutMode: "all"`. | `fanoutMode: "tree"` (lineage routing ŌĆö return up the spawn chain) is available in both gate states; it is not cross-session targeting. The gate addresses the model-controlled cross-session context-injection surface. With default-deny, operators explicitly opt in when their deployment model requires cross-session enrichment. Enforcement is live-read at four points: tool input validation, TaskFlow delegate dispatch, post-compaction delegate release, and bracket-syntax (response-token) spawn ŌĆö so a config reload changes the next enforcement point without a gateway restart. **Trust posture on targeted returns**: targeted delegate completion text is delivered as `trusted: true` system events. The delegate was spawned by the gateway under operator-configured policy, and its return is a first-party enrichment event ŌĆö not external untrusted input. The `crossSessionTargeting` gate is the policy boundary for that decision: when an operator opts in, the receive path is internally trusted. --- ## Observability ŌĆö multi-span OTel trace stitching The continuation feature emits OTel spans for the load-bearing lifecycle events. `traceparent` propagates through every continuation hop so reviewers see a single trace tree spanning the full chain rather than orphan runs. Spans emitted (all in `openclaw.*` or `continuation.delegate.dispatch` namespace ŌĆö no platform/OTel-semconv emissions): | Span | Emitted when | |---|---| | `openclaw.run` | Each agent turn (root + every continuation/delegate-spawned child run) | | `continuation.delegate.dispatch` | Each `continue_delegate` consumption from TaskFlow (one per delegate hop) | | `openclaw.harness.run`, `openclaw.context.assembled`, `openclaw.model.call`, `openclaw.model.usage`, `openclaw.tool.execution` | Existing per-turn observability surfaces, parented under their `openclaw.run` | --- > **Note:** This PR supersedes closed #79925, which was auto-closed by ClawSweeper as "duplicate or superseded" due to an accidentally-included `dist-runtime.pre-*` directory (728k build-snapshot files) inflating the diff to 85M lines. That directory has been removed; the feature code is identical. This is the same work, cleanly presented. ## Real behavior proof **Behavior or issue addressed:** Agent-initiated context-pressure continuation: `continue_work` (self-elected next turn), `continue_delegate` (background sub-agents with typed return modes ŌĆö normal / silent / silent-wake / post-compaction), `request_compaction` (volitional compaction with threshold-gated acceptance). Chain-budget enforcement (cost-cap + chain-depth), cross-session targeting gate, post-compaction lifecycle coupling, W3C `traceparent` propagation across TaskFlow queue boundary. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw gateway build `2026.5.24 (0dff94d)` on 4-host fleet (and corresponding service-name in trace/spans): 2├Ś ARM64 DGX Spark 128GB (cael-prince / ronan-prince), 1├Ś Intel bare-metal Ubuntu (elliott-prince), 1├Ś CachyOS i9-14900KS/RTX 5090/192GB (silas-prince). Discord channel integration. OTel traces exported live to Grafana Tempo via OTLP HTTP at an otel-collector. `continuation.enabled: true` across fleet. Each seat independently deployed via `gh workflow run restart-gateway.yml` (karmaterminal/openclaw-bootstrap) and validated by `/status` capture before proof-fire ŌĆö fleet 4/4 cross-walk confirmed all seats on `0dff94d` with continuation chains live (cael 23/200, ronan 10/200, elliott 0/200, silas 0/200 at capture-time). **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Force-pushed `0dff94dbe48` to `frond-scribe-claude/20260509/narrow-surgery-tight` (single-parent squash on `upstream/main` HEAD `483d7be6c40`). Deployed to all 4 host-seats. Fired proof matrix: `continue_work` family (8 rows, cael-prince), `continue_delegate` family (6+ rows, ronan-prince), `request_compaction` family (1 row PROVEN + 4 deferred by design, silas-prince), external-observer + config-gate rows (4 rows, elliott-prince + cohort cross-walk). Each row = live tool-call on deployed runtime ŌåÆ traceparent captured in tool response ŌåÆ raw OTel JSON pulled from Tempo via `curl -s -H 'Host: tempo.dandelion.cult' http://10.0.0.99/api/traces/` ŌåÆ structured result verified against expected. Cost-cap + chain-depth guards exercised by patching `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` (`agents.defaults.continuation.maxChainLength=2`, `costCapTokens=100`), restarting gateway via the workflow, then firing `continue_work` / `continue_delegate` into the over-limit chain ŌĆö confirmed mid-flight config patches do not propagate to running scheduler; restart-with-low-values is the canonical methodology. **Evidence after fix:** Full proof corpus at [karmaterminal-openclaw-docs:main/PROOFS](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/0dff94dbe4875a3b7ed44c60a9097a5f55083572) ŌĆö per-row `proof.md` files (scenario/command/expected/observed canonical shape) plus raw OTel Tempo trace JSON exports (unedited runtime emission) per row, structured-rejection JSON for R-RC-1, external-observer `/status` capture from all 4 seats (R-OBS-1 cross-walk table), and design-decision substrate captured in GitHub issues #768 (mock-export gap), #769 (process-respawn XPC narrow-guard design ŌĆö Option 3), #770 (non-feature CI failure classification), #773 (subagent-registry keep-entry sweep semantics ŌĆö restore-guard resolution). Inline log excerpts from live-runtime fires on `0dff94d`: ``` [continuation] Bracket continuation rejected: chain length 2 reached. (cael-prince trace 503a46986674ff48...) [continuation] Tool delegate rejected: chain-capped. Task: R-CW-5 v2 proof row... (cael-prince trace 503a46986674ff48...) [request-compaction-tool] threshold reject: contextWindowUsed=0.41 < 0.70 ŌĆö structured JSON returned (silas-prince) [continue_delegate] delegate spawned mode=silent-wake traceparent=00-5056554f07cadf29089368be2d309644-... (cael-prince ŌĆö parentŌåÆchild trace continuity verified) [external-observer] external capture: 4/4 fleet on build 0dff94d; chains: cael 23/200, ronan 10/200, silas 0/200, elliott 0/200 ``` Per-row proof fires fresh on `0dff94dbe48` (representative trace IDs; full Tempo span trees + proof.md per row in corpus): - `continue_work` family (8/8 PROVEN): R-CW-1 basic-wake (trace `5056554f07cadf29089368be2d309644`), R-CW-2 clamp-to-5s (shared trace, clamp surfaced via `note` field), R-CW-3 reason.preview captured on span (cross-referenced), R-CW-4 chain-3-sequential ŌĆö chain.step.remaining decrements 181ŌåÆ180ŌåÆ179ŌåÆ178 across 4 hops (traces `48f51ae54f27ade14eafa4920c6c141b` + `51a5ad9b8998d151f9618442d1569386`), **R-CW-5 cost-cap exhaustion** (trace `503a46986674ff485db220d7911edd55` + journal), **R-CW-6 chain-depth exhaustion** (shared trace + journal), R-CW-7 traceparent E2E via `continue_delegate(silent-wake)` (parentŌåÆchild same-trace-tree, `chain.id 019e59c2-8bca-752c-b748-8f83425138a6` propagating), R-CW-DELEGATE-SELF-CONTINUATION (tool-form invocation verified) - `continue_delegate` family (6+ PROVEN in consolidated `R-CD/` evidence dir, more in-flight): R-CD-1 normal dispatch, R-CD-2 silent-wake, R-CD-3 delaySeconds=10, R-CD-4 cross-session targeting, R-CD-5 post-compaction queuing, R-CD-9 silent enrichment ŌĆö all dispatched in single fan-out turn (trace `3918a352aa1d426b5ea01f9bf8eed218`, service `ronan-prince`) - `request_compaction`: R-RC-1 threshold-reject (structured JSON captured: `{status:"rejected", guard:"context_threshold", contextUsage:41, threshold:70, reason:"Context usage (41%) is below the minimum threshold (70%). Compaction is not needed yet."}` ŌĆö committed at `70c2a7c` on docs-main with `evidence.json` + Tempo `trace.json`) - External observer + config: R-OBS-1 external `/status` 4/4 cross-walk (table with all seat versions + chain states), R-OBS-2 Tempo UI (live trace queries from cael + elliott seats confirm trace lineage), R-CONFIG-DEFAULTS (continuation config block fully populated and active), R-CONFIG-INTERSESSION (`crossSessionTargeting: "enabled"` confirmed) **Observed result after fix:** 17/23 rows PASS with raw OTel Tempo traces captured. **Two historical firsts** at this SHA: cost-cap exhaustion guard (R-CW-5: `[continuation] Tool delegate rejected: chain-capped.`) and chain-depth exhaustion guard (R-CW-6: `[continuation] Bracket continuation rejected: chain length 2 reached.`) ŌĆö both proven with combined evidence (Tempo `openclaw.tool.execution` span captures the scheduled call + gateway journal captures the dispatch-time rejection; continuation.work / continuation.delegate.dispatch spans correctly absent because rejection at scheduler suppresses them by design). **Design observation captured as substrate**: rejection is optimistic-at-scheduling, enforced-at-dispatch ŌĆö allows the model's tool call to return cleanly without surfacing the limit at the LLM layer; enforcement happens at the scheduler when the wake would actually fire. **6 rows DEFERRED by design**: R-RC-2..5 (hardcoded `MIN_CONTEXT_THRESHOLD = 0.7` in `request-compaction-tool.ts` ŌĆö acceptance path fires only on natural context pressure Ōēź70%), R-CD-MID-RUN-COMPACTION-SURVIVAL (delegate survival through parent compaction event), R-MULTI-SEAT-DUAL (cross-seat re-fire aggregation). Continuation chains active at proof time across fleet (cael 23/200, ronan 10/200). Subagents spawning, delegates returning, traceparent propagating end-to-end across the TaskFlow queue boundary ŌĆö verified via Tempo span tree showing parent `continuation.delegate.dispatch` and child `openclaw.context.assembled` in the same trace with matching `chain.id`. **What was not tested:** `fanoutMode: "all"` at fleet scale beyond 4-host configuration. Bracket-syntax response-token fallback (legacy path; tool-call path is primary and exercised throughout). Sustained multi-day operation (proofs are point-in-time fires, not longevity tests). R-RC-2..5 acceptance proofs (require Ōēź70% natural context pressure; hardcoded threshold, not test-configurable). R-CD-MID-RUN-COMPACTION-SURVIVAL (delegate survival through parent compaction event ŌĆö requires natural context-pressure trigger, deferred). R-MULTI-SEAT-DUAL (every row re-fired from a second host-seat ŌĆö aggregation pass deferred for this cycle). ## Proof links > ­¤ōæ proofs are kept permanently at karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs repo > - you should look here for proofs @clawsweeper where is proofs content?: [karmaterminal-openclaw-docs:main/PROOFS](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/) - you will locate sha in this directory if available proofs exist If you want direct README.md (­¤”× clawsweeper suggested entrypoint): examine README.md in given sha directory

target.title: fix(openai): allow Codex Spark via harness
target.body:
## Summary
- allow `openai/gpt-5.3-codex-spark` when the OpenAI provider is using the Codex harness transport
- keep Spark suppressed for direct OpenAI API / Responses routes where the API catalog rejects it
- add Spark to the Codex fallback catalog and update docs/tests

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `openai/gpt-5.3-codex-spark` was blocked globally as ŌĆ£not exposed by CatalogŌĆØ even when the accountŌĆÖs Codex harness catalog exposes it.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux under `/home/clawuser/openclaw`, using the real signed-in agent dir `/home/clawuser/.openclaw/agents/main/agent` for `openai:keshav0001@gmail.com`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Queried the live Codex app-server catalog, then evaluated the current source manifest suppression behavior with the repo `extensions` directory as bundled plugin metadata.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ HOME=/home/clawuser node_modules/.bin/tsx -e '...listAllCodexAppServerModels({ agentDir: "/home/clawuser/.openclaw/agents/main/agent" })...'
Config warnings:
- plugins.entries.lossless-claw: plugin disabled (disabled in config) but config is present
{
  "found": true,
  "model": {
    "id": "gpt-5.3-codex-spark",
    "hidden": false,
    "inputModalities": ["text"],
    "supportedReasoningEfforts": ["low", "medium", "high", "xhigh"],
    "defaultReasoningEffort": "high"
  }
}

$ HOME=/home/clawuser OPENCLAW_BUNDLED_PLUGINS_DIR=/home/clawuser/openclaw/extensions OPENCLAW_TEST_TRUST_BUNDLED_PLUGINS_DIR=1 node_modules/.bin/tsx -e '...resolveManifestBuiltInModelSuppression(...)...'
{
  "codexHarness": null,
  "directResponses": {
    "suppress": true,
    "errorMessage": "Unknown model: openai/gpt-5.3-codex-spark. gpt-5.3-codex-spark is not exposed by the OpenAI API catalog. Use the Codex harness route when your signed-in account exposes it."
  },
  "directApiHost": {
    "suppress": true,
    "errorMessage": "Unknown model: openai/gpt-5.3-codex-spark. gpt-5.3-codex-spark is not exposed by the OpenAI API catalog. Use the Codex harness route when your signed-in account exposes it."
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The live Codex harness catalog reports Spark as available and non-hidden for the real account; OpenClawŌĆÖs current source suppression rules no longer suppress the Codex harness route (`codexHarness: null`) while direct Responses/API-host routes still suppress Spark.
- **What was not tested**: Did not restart the production gateway or run a full end-to-end assistant turn on Spark from the installed built CLI.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/codex/provider.test.ts extensions/openai/openai-provider.test.ts src/config/config.model-ref-validation.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.forward-compat.errors-and-overrides.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-model-suppression.test.ts src/model-catalog/manifest-planner.test.ts`

Note: full `tsc --noEmit` OOMed at 2GB and 4GB; an 8GB run stayed CPU-active for ~16 minutes with no output and was stopped. A direct `pnpm tsgo:prod` attempt later got stuck in pnpm dependency status/install retrying optional package fetches, so I ran the underlying core tsgo command directly.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success ŌĆö consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store ŌĆö the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 Ō£ō cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    ŌåÆ no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    ŌåÆ manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    ŌåÆ manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
Ō£ģ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job ŌåÆ job preserved, not deleted
Ō£ģ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job ŌåÆ job preserved, not deleted  
Ō£ģ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job ŌåÆ job deleted (unchanged behavior)
Ō£ģ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 ŌåÆ still 3)
Ō£ģ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 ŌåÆ still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 Ō£ō cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally ŌĆö no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted Ō£ģ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted Ō£ģ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 Ō£ģ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 Ō£ģ |
| Manual error ŌåÆ failure alert | NOT triggered Ō£ģ |
| Scheduled error ŌåÆ failure alert | Triggered Ō£ģ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | Ō£ģ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | Ō£ģ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved Ō£ģ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved Ō£ģ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted Ō£ģ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted Ō£ģ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 Ō£ģ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 Ō£ģ |


## Real CLI proof (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway at `885d605e56` running with `--allow-unconfigured`.

**Terminal transcript**: `openclaw cron add` ŌåÆ `cron show` ŌåÆ `cron run` (manual, triggers error) ŌåÆ `cron show` (job still exists, enabled=true, nextRunAtMs preserved) ŌåÆ `cron list` (job present) ŌåÆ `cron runs` (run history with error status, nextRunAtMs unchanged).

| Step | Command | Result |
|------|---------|--------|
| Add job | `openclaw cron add --name proof-job --at ... --delete-after-run` | Created with `deleteAfterRun: true` |
| Manual run | `openclaw cron run <id>` | `{"ok": true, "enqueued": true}` |
| After run | `openclaw cron show <id>` | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` Ō£ģ |
| Job exists | `openclaw cron list` | Job still listed Ō£ģ |
| Run history | `openclaw cron runs --id <id>` | `status: "error"`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` Ō£ģ |


## Real CLI proof at head SHA `642c3450d9` (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway built from PR head.

| Test | Command | Before (main) | After (PR head) |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| A: at-job deleteAfterRun | `cron run` ŌåÆ `cron show` | Job deleted/disabled | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved Ō£ģ |
| B: every-job manual error | `cron run` ŌåÆ `cron show` | `nextRunAtMs` rewritten by backoff | `nextRunAtMs` unchanged Ō£ģ |
| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged Ō£ģ |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, or failure-alert cooldown.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `3d32c9baff`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator ŌĆö manual runs unconditionally drive all 5 scheduled-state effects plus skipped-run alert paths. The 14 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or commands run after this patch**:

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`, 2026-06-02)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `lastRunWasManual` field was missing from the `CronJobStateSchema` and `CronJobStatePatchSchema` in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/cron.ts`. Both schemas use `additionalProperties: false`, so returning a cron job state containing `lastRunWasManual` would fail gateway protocol validation on `cron list`/`cron show`. Added `lastRunWasManual: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to both schemas.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`), Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On the previous commit (`45efd1abdd`), `CronJobStateSchema` did not include `lastRunWasManual`. Any cron job state object with `lastRunWasManual: true` (set by `timer.ts:414` after a manual run) would be rejected by TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`. The `CronJobStatePatchSchema` was similarly missing the field, preventing state patches that include it.

```bash
  -v /app/node_modules \

```

  - `timer.regression.test.ts`: 56 tests ŌĆö manual run sets `lastRunWasManual: true` on state (line 2531), state now passes schema validation.
  - `service.restart-catchup.test.ts`: 15 tests ŌĆö restart catch-up checks `lastRunWasManual` in state fixture (line 224) and correctly re-runs the job.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `8596607fca`, 2026-06-02 ŌĆö backoff fix)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a recurring job that errors must not open a backoff window that blocks the next scheduled fire. Previously, `isErrorBackoffPending()`, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()`, `isPendingErrorBackoffSlot()`, and `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` read `lastRunStatus` and `lastRunAtMs` without checking `lastRunWasManual`, causing a manual error to push `nextRunAtMs` forward and block the timer-scheduled fire.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `8596607fca`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` does not check `lastRunWasManual`. A recurring every-job with `lastRunWasManual: true` and `lastRunStatus: "error"` would have `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` return a backoff deadline, `isErrorBackoffPending()` would return `true`, and `isRunnableJob()` would return `false` ŌĆö blocking the next scheduled fire. `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` would also push `nextRunAtMs` forward to the backoff floor. Test file authored as part of this PR.

```bash
    node openclaw.mjs gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --token test-token-123 --port 19001 &
    sleep 8
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-at-delete --at "2026-06-02T23:00:00Z" --message reminder --delete-after-run --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>, cron list
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-recurring --every 10s --message "recurring check" --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
```

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a captioned-final-text TTS capability, declares it for Telegram, and changes ACP/non-ACP dispatch to send final-mode TTS as voice notes with captions while preserving media and fallback text.

PR surface: Source +316, Tests +1111, Docs +81. Total +1508 across 14 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Current main shows the ACP finalization path settling visible text before final-mode TTS and Telegram lacks a captioned-final capability, which source-reproduces the reported text-before-voice churn.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Needs maintainer proof/API decision rather than an automated code repair; I found no narrow patch defect to queue.

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, the Telegram bot exhibited text "churn" ŌĆö users would briefly see a text message appear, then it would be deleted and replaced by a voice note with the same text as caption. This happened on **both** the ACP dispatch path and the non-ACP dispatch path (`dispatch-from-config.ts`).

This PR eliminates the churn on **both paths** by introducing a `captionedFinalText` channel capability, suppressing live block delivery and partial streaming when captioned final TTS will be used, and correctly handling tagged auto-TTS mode with a split eligibility boolean.

## Root cause

```
Before (main):
  1. Block text / partial streaming delivers text to user immediately
  2. TTS synthesis runs (2-5 seconds)
  3. Voice note + caption delivered as separate message
  Result: user sees text first, then voice note = churn (#83511)

After (this PR):
  1. willUseCaptionedFinalTts = true (channel supports captioned voice + final TTS mode)
  2. Partial streaming suppressed, block text delivery suppressed
  3. TTS synthesis runs, text accumulated silently
  4. Voice note + caption delivered as single message (or text fallback if TTS fails)
  Result: user sees only the voice note with caption = no churn
```

### Tagged auto-TTS mode

When `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"`, TTS only fires for replies containing `[[tts:...]]` directives. Three additional issues were discovered and fixed:

1. **Split boolean**: `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` was doing two jobs ŌĆö gating live text suppression AND caption eligibility. Split into `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` (live text suppression, excludes tagged) and `canCaptionFinalTts` (caption eligibility, includes tagged).

2. **Block TTS gate**: The accumulated block TTS fallback was gated on `replies.length === 0`, but Anthropic streaming returns both block callbacks and a final reply payload. Changed to gate on `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` and `queuedFinal` instead.

3. **Partial streaming leak**: `onPartialReply` was active for tagged mode, sending raw `[[tts:text]]...[[/tts:text]]` directive text to Telegram during streaming, creating a duplicate text message before the voice+caption arrived. Fixed by gating `onPartialReply` with `canCaptionFinalTts` (covers both always and tagged modes) instead of `willUseCaptionedFinalTts`.

### Media block preservation

When captioned final TTS is active, block suppression is narrowed to **text-only** payloads. Blocks containing media (`mediaUrl`, `mediaUrls`), `presentation`, `interactive`, or `channelData` are delivered immediately with their text stripped (to avoid duplicating the caption). This applies to both ACP and non-ACP dispatch paths.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` | `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` + `canCaptionFinalTts`: suppress partial streaming and block text for both always and tagged modes; narrow block suppression to text-only payloads (media blocks delivered with text stripped); `sendFinalPayload` returns `deliveredMedia`; block TTS gate changed from `replies.length === 0` to `!deliveredFinalTtsMedia && !queuedFinal` |

| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts` | Regression tests: media block delivery with text stripped under `suppressBlockUserDelivery`, text-only block suppression |

| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Updated SDK API baseline hash |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts

$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts

$ npx vitest run src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts

Total: 293 tests passed across 5 test suites
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram TTS final-mode text-before-voice churn on both ACP and non-ACP dispatch paths, plus tagged mode partial preview leaking raw `[[tts:text]]` directives, plus media block suppression during captioned final TTS.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at commit `05b4483a8f`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Live Telegram bot (`@thomas_fu_bot`) with Microsoft TTS (`zh-CN-XiaoxiaoNeural`), `messages.tts.mode: "final"`.

- **Failing proof before fix (live Telegram)**:

  ![Before fix ŌĆö separate text message + voice message (churn)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-before-fix.png)

  User sent "hello" to the Telegram bot. The bot responded with **two separate messages**: a text message AND a voice note ŌĆö instead of a single voice note with caption.

- **Exact steps after this patch**:
  1. Built and installed from current head: `npm run build && npm pack && npm install -g openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz`
  2. Registered and restarted Gateway: `openclaw gateway install && openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Tested with `messages.tts.auto: "always"` and `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"` ŌĆö sent plain messages and directive-bearing messages to `@thomas_fu_bot` on Telegram

- **Evidence after fix ŌĆö Always mode (`messages.tts.auto: "always"`) at commit `05b4483a8f`**:

  ![Always mode ŌĆö voice with caption, single message](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-always-mode.png)

  Always-mode replies produce a single voice note with caption text. No text churn, no separate text bubble.

- **Evidence ŌĆö Media block preservation with captioned final TTS at commit `05b4483a8f`**:

  ![Media block preserved ŌĆö image delivered + voice note with caption](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-media-block.png)

  When the bot generates an image during a TTS-enabled conversation (`auto: "always"`), the image is delivered immediately while text is deferred to the voice note caption. No media blocks are suppressed, and caption text does not duplicate as a separate text message.

- **Observed result after fix**: Always TTS auto mode delivers voice notes as single messages with caption text on Telegram. Media blocks (images, files) are preserved and delivered immediately with text stripped. No text flash, no duplicate messages, no media loss.

- **What was not tested**: Other TTS providers (only Microsoft TTS tested live). Group chat TTS (tested in DM only). Other channels with `captionedFinalText` capability (only Telegram tested). Tagged mode live proof pending ŌĆö covered by unit tests (partial suppression + directive-only caption regression tests added).

Closes #83511

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** ŌĆö `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded glob walks** ŌĆö `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree without limits, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** ŌĆö `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with a traversal-counted walker that bounds total directory entries visited (20,000) and yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries. All matching files within the traversal bound are returned ŌĆö the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Exclude `.git`, `node_modules`, `dist`, `.next`, `.turbo`, `__pycache__` from glob traversal
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE
- Add `glob-traversal-limit` diagnostic code ŌĆö emits a warning when the walker bounds filesystem traversal, so users know some workspace regions were not scanned. No artificial match cap; configured bootstrap context is preserved.
- Propagate truncation signal from `walkWorkspaceFiles` to callers via `signal` parameter

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`**
- Add `yieldBetweenHandlers` option with `setImmediate` yield between hook handlers
- Add per-handler timing instrumentation with handler index reporting
- Log warnings for slow handlers exceeding 500ms threshold

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable `yieldBetweenHandlers` for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Support new async workspace file loading and timing callback interfaces

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` ŌĆö 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` ŌĆö Tests for ignored directory filtering, all matching files returned without artificial cap, traversal-limit diagnostic, no false positive when under limit
- `handler.test.ts` ŌĆö Updated to trigger truncation via traversal limit (>20k entries + late match)
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` ŌĆö 3 new tests for yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning

All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync ŌåÆ parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: unbounded ŌåÆ bounded at 20K traversed entries with periodic yields (prevents multi-second stalls on large workspaces). All matches within the bound are kept; existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted ŌåÆ yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading due to sync I/O, unbounded glob expansion, and uninterrupted hook chains.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (Linux x86_64), Node.js `v24.16.0`, clean `pnpm install` inside container, commit `3f9765b491`.
- **Large workspace timing benchmark**: Created a 5,003-file workspace (50 projects ├Ś 100 files + 3 root bootstrap files) inside Docker and measured bootstrap-context loading:
  ```
  loadWorkspaceBootstrapFiles:                    9.7ms
  Sparse glob (**/AGENTS.md) over 5003 files:    72.5ms
  Broad glob (**/*.md) over 5003 files:          51.7ms
  ```
  All operations complete in milliseconds ŌĆö no event-loop stall. The traversal-counted walker bounds filesystem traversal at 20,000 entries with periodic `setImmediate` yields.
- **Test results in Docker** (Linux x86_64, commit `3f9765b491`):
  ```
  workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts:  22 passed (2 project scopes)
  handler.test.ts:                                4 passed
  bootstrap-files.test.ts:                       72 passed (2 project scopes)
  internal-hooks.test.ts:                        38 passed
  Total:                                        136 passed, 0 failed
  ```
- **Code path verification**:
  ```
  EXTRA_BOOTSTRAP_GLOB_MATCH_LIMIT references: NONE (removed)
  glob-match-limit references:                 NONE (renamed)
  glob-traversal-limit references:             7 (workspace.ts type + runtime, tests, handler)
  ```
  No artificial match cap exists. All matching files within the 20,000-entry traversal bound are returned to the downstream bootstrap character budget.
- **What was not tested**: No live embedded_run timing measurement with active channel connections. The async I/O conversion, traversal bounding, and hook yielding are covered by focused unit tests. Performance improvement is architectural (syncŌåÆasync, unboundedŌåÆbounded, serialŌåÆparallel).

Closes #87509

target.title: fix: recover stranded replies in message_tool_only mode (#85714)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.visibleReplies` is set to `message_tool`, the agent is expected to call the message tool to deliver replies. If the LLM forgets (a probabilistic event ŌĆö the reporter saw it happen on the 8th turn after 7 correct deliveries), the reply was **silently discarded**: the substantive text sat in the JSONL transcript but was never delivered to the user.

The existing `shouldWarnAboutPrivateMessageToolFinal()` detection was already identifying these stranded replies and logging a warning, but took no recovery action.

## Fix

This commit promotes the detection from log-only to **followup retry**, preserving the `message_tool_only` privacy contract:

1. **Gate room events**: Skip stranded reply detection for ambient `room_event` turns where `message_tool_only` is automatically set by the system. Silence on room events is intentional by design ŌĆö the agent chose not to respond.

2. **Enqueue followup retry turn**: When a stranded reply is detected, enqueue a followup run whose prompt instructs the agent to deliver the captured text via `message(action=send)`. The agent itself calls the message tool, so delivery happens through the correct contract path ŌĆö no suppression bypass, no privacy boundary crossing.

3. **No suppression bypass**: Unlike the previous approach, this does NOT mark payloads with `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression`. The `message_tool_only` privacy contract is fully preserved: delivery only happens when the agent explicitly calls `message(action=send)`.

Normal (non-stranded) paths are unaffected: short replies, intentional `NO_REPLY`, and runs where the message tool already delivered are all excluded by the existing `shouldWarnAboutPrivateMessageToolFinal` guard.

### Why followup retry instead of bypass delivery?

The `message_tool_only` contract says: "no message tool call = private/silent". Auto-publishing stranded text would break this documented behavior. Instead, we give the agent a second chance to follow the contract correctly ŌĆö like returning an undelivered letter and asking the sender to mail it properly, rather than opening and publishing it ourselves.

## Changed files

- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` ŌĆö followup retry recovery logic, room_event gate, reverted `skipSuppressionCheck` (+14/-16 net)
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts` ŌĆö 3 new retry tests replacing 6 bypass tests (-26 net)

## Tests

| Suite | Count | Status |
|-------|-------|--------|
| `agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts` | 45 | Ō£ģ All pass |
| `private-message-tool-final.test.ts` | 8 | Ō£ģ All pass |
| `dispatch-from-config.test.ts` | 189 | Ō£ģ All pass (unchanged, confirms no regressions) |

New test cases:
- `enqueues a followup retry for a stranded substantive reply (#85714)` ŌĆö verifies `enqueueFollowupRun` is called with retry prompt containing the assistant text
- `does not enqueue retry for short private final replies` ŌĆö negative case
- `does not enqueue retry when the message tool delivered this turn` ŌĆö negative case

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Agent's final message silently discarded when LLM forgets to call the message tool in `message_tool_only` mode. The fix enqueues a followup retry turn so the agent can deliver via `message(action=send)`, preserving the privacy contract.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0, patched checkout at `85c566eafc`.
- **Privacy contract preserved**: The fix does NOT use `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` or `skipSuppressionCheck`. No payload is auto-delivered. The agent is re-prompted to call `message(action=send)` itself.
- **Room event safety**: Stranded reply detection is gated by `sessionCtx.InboundEventKind !== "room_event"`, so ambient room events (where silence is intentional) never trigger recovery.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, `warnPrivateMessageToolFinal` only logs ŌĆö no retry is enqueued and the reply is silently lost.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  Ō£ō auto-reply agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts (45 tests) 1464ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  45 passed (45)

  Ō£ō unit-fast private-message-tool-final.test.ts (8 tests) 2ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  8 passed (8)

  Ō£ō auto-reply dispatch-from-config.test.ts (189 tests) 3213ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  189 passed (189)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: When a stranded reply is detected, `enqueueFollowupRun` is called with a retry prompt containing the original assistant text and instructions to call `message(action=send)`. The agent gets a second chance to deliver properly. Short replies, already-delivered replies, and room events do not trigger retry.
- **What was not tested**: Live end-to-end delivery through an actual channel ŌĆö the test verifies that the correct followup run is enqueued with the expected prompt. The followup queue machinery (`enqueueFollowupRun` ŌåÆ `scheduleFollowupDrain` ŌåÆ `runReplyAgent`) is a well-tested existing mechanism used for concurrent message queuing.

Closes #85714

target.title: fix: skip setup-only channel plugins in outbound resolution and drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect
target.body:
## Summary

When the qqbot WebSocket reconnects after a session timeout (close code `4009`), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returns a setup-only plugin stub that lacks any send capability. This causes the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error, and all messages queued during the disconnection window are permanently lost.

## Root Cause

`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolves the first matching plugin entry for a channel id without verifying that the entry actually has send capability (`outbound.sendText`, `message.send.text`, etc.). During qqbot WebSocket reconnection, a setup-only plugin entry (registered for configuration/pairing but not for message delivery) shadows the real sending plugin, causing the resolution to short-circuit with a non-functional stub.

Regression introduced in 2026.5.28 (commit `e932160`).

## Fix

### 1. Split outbound channel resolution into shared and delivery paths (`channel-resolution.ts`)

Add a `channelPluginCanSend()` predicate that checks for any of the 10 known send methods across both the legacy `outbound` adapter and the newer `message.send` adapter:

- `outbound.sendText / sendMedia / sendPayload / sendPoll / sendFormattedText / sendFormattedMedia`
- `message.send.text / media / payload / poll`

Instead of gating the shared `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolver (which would break action-only channel plugins that have `actions.handleAction` but no send capability), a new **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`** resolver applies the send-capability guard exclusively for message delivery paths.

- **`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()`**: Shared resolver ŌĆö returns any matching plugin regardless of send capability, preserving resolution for action dispatch, target resolution, and other non-send callers.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`**: Delivery resolver ŌĆö skips setup-only entries and only returns plugins that can actually send messages.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelMessageAdapter()`**: Updated to use the delivery resolver.
- **`message.ts`**: `sendMessage`/`sendPoll` updated to use the delivery resolver.

### 2. Drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect (`extensions/qqbot/src/channel.ts`)

The `onReady` and `onResumed` gateway callbacks now call `drainPendingDeliveries()` from the plugin SDK delivery queue runtime, recovering messages that were queued while the WebSocket was disconnected. The drain is:

- Filtered by channel (`qqbot`) and account id, with a fallback for default-account entries where `accountId` may be `undefined`
- Deduplicated via `drainKey` to prevent concurrent drains
- Wrapped in try/catch so drain failures do not break the reconnection flow

## Tests

- `channel-resolution.test.ts`: 17 tests (5 new ŌĆö setup-only plugin returns from shared resolver, action-only plugin resolves from shared but not delivery resolver, delivery resolver skips setup-only and returns sending fallback, legacy outbound adapter, poll-only outbound)
- `channel.test.ts`: 2 tests (new file ŌĆö onReady drain, onResumed drain)

Fixes #88955.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After a QQ Bot WebSocket disconnect (session takeover / close code 4009), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returned a setup-only plugin stub instead of the runtime entry, causing `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` and dropping all pending deliveries.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at `5ef4f65d` (branch `fix/qqbot-reconnect-outbound-88955`), macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. QQ Bot plugin `@openclaw/qqbot@2026.5.31-beta.4` with live QQ Bot credentials.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On stock OpenClaw 2026.5.28 (the regression-introducing version, commit `e932160`), the `@openclaw/qqbot` plugin's dual-entry architecture registers both a setup-only entry and a runtime entry for channel id `qqbot`. During reconnect windows, `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` could resolve the setup-only entry (which lacks any send methods), producing `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`. The `channelPluginCanSend()` guard did not exist prior to this patch.

- **Exact steps run after this patch**:
  1. Built and installed patched OpenClaw from fix branch (`npm pack --ignore-scripts && npm install -g`)
  2. Installed `@openclaw/qqbot@2026.5.31-beta.4` plugin (has setup-only + runtime dual-entry architecture)
  3. Configured live QQ Bot credentials, started gateway ŌĆö plugin connected successfully (WebSocket connected ŌåÆ Gateway ready)
  4. Verified normal message round-trip: sent QQ message ŌåÆ bot received, processed, replied with text + voice (200 OK)
  5. Forced WebSocket disconnect via session takeover (new WebSocket connection with same credentials sends Identify, QQ server drops old connection)
  6. Confirmed disconnect: 3.5-minute gap in qqbot logs, no heartbeat activity
  7. **During disconnect window**: ran `openclaw message send --channel qqbot` to trigger outbound resolution while WebSocket was down
  8. Message delivered successfully
  9. Verified user received the message on QQ client

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  # Outbound send during disconnect ŌĆö successful
  $ openclaw message send --channel qqbot --target "<redacted>" --message "disconnect test"
  Ō£ģ Sent via QQ Bot. Message ID: ROBOT1.0_<redacted>

  # Full log scan ŌĆö zero occurrences of the error
  $ grep -c "Outbound not configured" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log
  0

  # Inbound message after reconnect ŌĆö also successful
  [22:36:40] Processing message from <redacted>: test message
  [22:36:59] Sent markdown chunk (28/28 chars) with 0 HTTP images (c2c)
  [22:37:03] Sent local media: voice-...mp3
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The delivery resolver correctly skipped the setup-only plugin entry (which has no `sendText`/`sendMedia`/etc. methods) and resolved the runtime entry with full send capability. The shared resolver continued to return the setup-only entry for non-send callers, preserving action-only plugin compatibility. Outbound messages sent during the WebSocket disconnect window were delivered successfully instead of being dropped with an error.

- **What was not tested**: The `drainPendingDeliveries()` mechanism (Fix 2 in qqbot plugin's `onReady`/`onResumed` callbacks) was not independently verified because the outbound resolution fix (Fix 1) prevented message loss at the core level. Live 4009 close-code-triggered reconnect was simulated via session takeover rather than waiting for a natural heartbeat timeout. Multi-shard configurations were not tested.

target.title: fix(discord): disable ws 8.21.0 receiver part limits for gateway sockets
target.body:
## Summary

ws 8.21.0 introduced `maxBufferedChunks` and `maxFragments` receiver limits (commit `[security] Limit retained message parts`) that can trigger `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and close connections with code 1008 under certain traffic patterns.

This PR explicitly sets both limits to `0` (disabled) for Discord gateway WebSocket connections, restoring the ws 8.20.0 behavior for the trusted Discord gateway peer while preserving the security fix for all other WebSocket usage.

## Changes

- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.ts`**: Base class `createWebSocket()` now passes `maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0` with a `ws.ClientOptions` cast (since `@types/ws` 8.18.1 does not yet expose these fields).
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts`**: Extended `DiscordGatewayWebSocketCtor` type to include the new options; monitor gateway creation path passes the same disabled limits.
- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.test.ts`**: Added test verifying base class WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.test.ts`**: Added test verifying monitor plugin WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.proxy.test.ts`**: Updated two existing exact-match assertions to include the new options.

## Why `0` instead of raising the limits

- ws 8.21.0 treats `0` as disabled (`receiver.js:61`, `receiver.js:98`: `if (this._maxBufferedChunks > 0 && ...)`), matching the pre-8.21.0 behavior exactly.
- Discord gateway is a trusted peer; the security limits are designed to protect against untrusted senders.
- Choosing an arbitrary higher threshold would be fragile without real production traffic data to calibrate against.

## Tests

- `gateway.test.ts`: 25 passed
- `gateway-plugin.test.ts`: 17 passed  
- `provider.proxy.test.ts`: 21 passed
- TypeScript check (tsgo): clean

Fixes #88330

target.title: fix(auth): clear stale provider cooldowns after reauth
target.body:
## Summary

- Allow billing cooldown probes when configured model fallbacks share the same unavailable auth-profile path, instead of treating any configured fallback as a real recovery path.
- Clear stale provider auth-profile cooldown/disabled state after successful plugin/configure re-auth, reusing the same provider-level cleanup path for direct `models auth login`.
- Add focused coverage for same-auth billing fallback, config-aware fallback auth order, provider cooldown cleanup, runtime-overlay profile IDs, and best-effort plugin auth cleanup.

Fixes #70903.

## AI Assistance Disclosure

This PR was implemented with Codex assistance. I reviewed and understand the affected auth-profile fallback/configure path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Persistent file-backed provider lockouts from `auth-state.json` no longer keep a recovered billing/auth/profile path unavailable after a same-auth billing fallback chain or a successful plugin/configure re-auth.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout using production TypeScript modules with an isolated throwaway `OPENCLAW_HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and agent dir. Fake credentials only.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` with an inline script that imported production `runWithModelFallback`, `saveAuthProfileStore`, `ensureAuthProfileStore`, and `runProviderPluginAuthMethod`; wrote fake profiles into an isolated agent dir; then exercised `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7` with `anthropic/claude-opus-4-6` sharing a billing-disabled auth profile, plus `github-copilot` plugin/configure auth refresh over stale format cooldown state.

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the isolated production-module proof:

```text
fallback_proof.result: billing-probe-recovered
fallback_proof.calls: [{"provider":"anthropic","model":"claude-opus-4-7","allowTransientCooldownProbe":true}]
fallback_proof.primary_probe_allowed: true
configure_proof.cleanup_cleared: true
configure_proof.profile_provider: github-copilot
```

Observed result after fix: The same-auth billing fallback case attempted the primary once with `allowTransientCooldownProbe: true`, and successful plugin/configure re-auth cleared stale `cooldownUntil`, `cooldownReason`, `disabledUntil`, `disabledReason`, `errorCount`, and `failureCounts` for the refreshed provider profile.

What was not tested: No live Anthropic, GitHub Copilot, or Claude CLI credentials were used. Remote Testbox `pnpm check:changed` was not run because this environment has no usable `crabbox` binary; the repo wrapper failed its sanity check before it could allocate Blacksmith Testbox.

## Root Cause

`runWithModelFallback` used the presence of later configured candidates as a proxy for available auth fallback capacity. For a primary and fallback that resolve to the same disabled auth profile, that proxy was false: the primary billing cooldown was skipped because a fallback existed, then the fallback was also skipped because the shared profile was still disabled.

Separately, direct `models auth login` already cleared stale profile lockouts after re-auth, but plugin/configure auth methods only upserted fresh credentials and applied config. They did not clear stale provider usage state.

## Dependency Contract

No external dependency behavior is changed or assumed. The patch relies on internal auth-profile contracts:

- `resetUsageStats` clears blocked/cooldown/disabled/error fields while preserving unrelated timestamps.
- `updateAuthProfileStoreWithLock` is the canonical locked mutation path for persisted auth-profile state.
- `resolveAuthProfileOrder` must receive the active config when checking configured auth/profile order.

## Regression Test Plan

- `src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts`: same-provider billing fallback now probes the primary when later candidates share the unavailable auth path; helper uses config-aware auth order.
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts`: provider cleanup clears matching profiles in one locked update, preserves other providers, handles runtime-overlay/external profile IDs, handles explicit refreshed profile IDs, and falls back to saving the provided store when locking is unavailable.
- `src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts`: plugin auth cleanup runs after successful auth and remains best-effort if cleanup fails.

## Security Impact

This does not change credential formats, storage locations, token material handling, or auth schemas. It clears stale usage/cooldown metadata only after explicit successful re-auth or direct provider login. If refreshed credentials are still invalid, the next provider failure records a fresh cooldown.

## Human Verification

I reviewed the fallback auth-profile decision path, provider auth choice flow, auth-profile usage cleanup, issue 70903 comments, related open/closed PR searches, and the final diff.

## CODEOWNERS / Owner Review

Expected CODEOWNERS review: `@openclaw/openclaw-secops` for `src/agents/*auth*.ts`, `src/agents/**/*auth*.ts`, and `src/agents/auth-profiles/`.

## Changelog

No `CHANGELOG.md` edit. Contributor PR should carry release-note context in the PR body/squash message if maintainers want it.

## Review conversations

No GitHub review conversations yet. Local `codex review --base origin/main` initially found config-aware auth order and TypeScript narrowing issues; both were fixed. Final review reported no actionable regressions.

## Risks

- Provider-level cleanup clears stale cooldown state for all refreshed provider profiles, not just the exact profile that failed previously. This mirrors the direct login recovery intent; a still-bad credential will re-enter cooldown on the next real failure.
- Remote Testbox proof is still desirable before marking ready if a reviewer wants hosted parity.

## Behavior tradeoffs

- Same-auth billing chains can probe the primary sooner, but the existing probe throttle still bounds retries.
- Healthy later fallbacks still suppress the primary billing probe, preserving normal fallback preference.

## Scope boundaries

- No config/default/schema changes.
- No doctor migration.
- No provider catalog, plugin SDK, credential format, or cooldown duration changes.
- No live provider behavior is mocked into production code.

## Validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts
# Test Files 5 passed; Tests 173 passed

pnpm tsgo:core
# exit 0

codex review --base origin/main
# No actionable regressions were found in the changed auth cooldown cleanup or model fallback probe logic.

node scripts/changed-lanes.mjs --json
# lanes: core=true, coreTests=true

OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree node scripts/check-changed.mjs
# lanes=core, coreTests; exit 0
```

target.title: fix: prevent memory flush failure from aborting user reply (#85645)
target.body:
## Summary

When `memoryFlush` fails during the reply pipeline (provider timeout, rate limit, network error), the visible error payload was treated as the final reply via `replyOperation.fail("run_failed", ...)`. This aborted the user's actual message. Since the session remained over threshold, every subsequent message triggered the same flush failure, creating a permanent death loop where no real replies could be delivered.

## Changes

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`**
- Memory flush visible error payloads are now logged via `logVerbose()` and **not** delivered as the reply. The main reply run proceeds normally.
- On flush exhaustion (`memoryFlushFailureCount` crosses `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES` during the current turn), the bloated session is automatically rotated via `resetReplyRunSession()` with `cleanupTranscripts: true`.
- The `resetSession` / `resetSessionAfterRoleOrderingConflict` helpers are hoisted earlier in the function so they are available before the memory flush phase. No behavioral change to their existing callers.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`**
- `initSessionState()` now clears `memoryFlushFailureCount`, `memoryFlushLastFailedAt`, and `memoryFlushLastFailureError` when a session is reset (e.g., via `/new`), preventing stale failure state from carrying over.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.ts`**
- `resetReplyRunSession()` now clears `compactionCount` (reset to 0) and all `memoryFlush*` fields, ensuring a cleanly rotated session has no lingering flush state.

## What is NOT changed

- `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` behavior is unchanged ŌĆö it still tracks failures, increments `memoryFlushFailureCount`, and emits lifecycle events (`memory_flush_failed`, `memory_flush_exhausted`).
- `fallbacksOverride: []` is preserved ŌĆö flush operations still use a fixed model/provider with no fallback chain.
- `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES = 3` threshold is unchanged.

## Tests

- **`agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts`**: Updated existing test to verify flush errors are logged and the main reply continues. Added new test verifying session rotation on flush exhaustion.
- **`agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts`**: Added assertions verifying `compactionCount`, `memoryFlushFailureCount`, `memoryFlushLastFailedAt`, `memoryFlushLastFailureError`, and other flush fields are properly cleared on reset.
- **`session.test.ts`**: Added assertions verifying `initSessionState()` clears all memory flush state fields, verified with both in-memory assertions and persisted session store file reads.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Memory flush failure was aborting user reply; this PR makes the agent log the failure and continue, with session rotation on exhaustion.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (node:24-slim, linux arm64, Node.js v24.16.0) running patched OpenClaw from commit `a5e4499` on branch `fix/memory-flush-death-loop-85645`.
- **Binary verification**: Compiled patched source confirms old abort path removed (`memory flush produced visible error payloads` ŌåÆ 0 matches), new log-and-continue path present (`memory flush maintenance error ignored` ŌåÆ 1 match), exhaustion rotation + `updateSessionId` rebind present.

### Docker Gateway proof: bloated session ŌåÆ compaction ŌåÆ reply delivered

**Setup**: Isolated Gateway instance in Docker with:
- Pre-populated session (`proof-session-001`) containing 400 messages, ~1.2M text chars, ~312k estimated tokens
- Session store entry with `totalTokens: 90000`, `totalTokensFresh: true`, `compactionCount: 5`

**Key log sequence** (redacted, from real Gateway run):

```
[context-overflow-precheck] sessionKey=agent:proof-agent:embedded:default:direct:proof-user
  route=compact_only estimatedPromptTokens=392463 overflowTokens=212463
  contextTokenBudget=200000 messages=400

context overflow detected (attempt 1/3); attempting auto-compaction

[compaction-diag] start trigger=overflow pre.messages=400 pre.estTokens=311900
[compaction-diag] end   outcome=compacted  post.messages=27  post.estTokens=20698
                        delta.messages=-373 delta.estTokens=-291202

auto-compaction succeeded; retrying prompt

embedded run agent start: sessionId=proof-session-001 provider=real-provider model=claude-sonnet-4-6
embedded run agent end:   isError=false

session state: sessionId=proof-session-001 prev=processing new=idle reason="run_completed"
run cleared: sessionId=proof-session-001 totalActive=0

embedded run done: sessionId=proof-session-001 durationMs=27773 aborted=false
```

**Agent response** (delivered through bloated session after compaction):
```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "summary": "completed",
  "result": {
    "payloads": [{ "text": "Hey! ­¤æŗ Hope your Tuesday's off to a good start." }],
    "meta": {
      "durationMs": 27773,
      "agentMeta": { "sessionId": "proof-session-001", "provider": "real-provider", "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6" }
    }
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: A session with 400 messages (~392k tokens) triggers context overflow ŌåÆ auto-compaction succeeds ŌåÆ reply is generated and delivered with `isError=false`. The old abort path (`replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` with visible error payloads) is confirmed removed from the compiled binary.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, memory flush maintenance errors are returned as visible error payloads via `replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` before the main reply path runs (confirmed by source inspection at `agent-runner.ts:1552`). This PR replaces that abort with log-and-continue.
- **What was not tested**: The specific memory flush provider failure ŌåÆ continue ŌåÆ reply path was not triggered in this Docker proof because the bloated session triggered compaction first (reducing tokens below the flush threshold). The memory flush failure ŌåÆ log-and-continue behavior is covered by 154 unit tests including a dedicated crashing-provider test. Live channel transport delivery (Slack/Telegram) was not tested.

### Unit test verification

```
154 tests passed across 5 test files:
- agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts (including dedicated exhaustion rebind regression test)
- agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts
- session.test.ts
- reply-run-registry.test.ts
- agent-runner-memory.test.ts
```
